name: Izanami
description: Izanami-no-Mikoto, often simply referred to as Izanami, is a central figure in Japanese mythology and one of the primordial deities responsible for the creation of Japan. Along with her brother-husband Izanagi, she descended from the heavens to create the islands of Japan from the primordial chaos. She gave birth to many other deities who populated the land and established the natural world. Tragically, Izanami died giving birth to Kagutsuchi, the fire god. This event marked the end of her role in the active creation of the world. Devastated, Izanagi followed her to Yomi, the land of the dead, in an attempt to bring her back. However, he broke a promise not to look upon her in her decayed state, causing her great shame and anger. She then vowed to kill a thousand people a day to prevent the living from returning. This led to Izanagi's vow to create fifteen hundred people a day, thus the cycle of life and death began. Izanami became the ruler of Yomi, the underworld, and is associated with death and decay, but also with creation and motherhood, highlighting the cyclical nature of existence in Shinto beliefs. Her legacy is essential to understanding the origins of Japan and the fundamental principles of life, death, and rebirth.
subtitle: Creator Goddess and Queen of Yomi

keyMyths.title: Creation of Japan
keyMyths.description: Izanami and Izanagi created the islands of Japan by stirring the primordial ocean with a jeweled spear. The droplets that fell from the spear formed the islands.

keyMyths.title: Death of Izanami
keyMyths.description: Izanami died giving birth to the fire god Kagutsuchi, leading to Izanagi's journey to Yomi. Her death brought mortality to the world.

keyMyths.title: Izanagi in Yomi
keyMyths.description: Izanagi ventured into Yomi to retrieve Izanami, but his breaking of a promise led to their separation and her becoming the ruler of the underworld.
